findings.description: The PDF uses a free-form layout with time slots and topics arranged in a loosely columnar format without explicit table borders, while the HTML renders the agenda items in a formal table with visible row dividers and column headers.
findings.evidence: PDF shows agenda items as aligned text blocks without table borders. HTML wraps them in a structured table with 'Time', 'Topic', and 'Presenter' column headers and horizontal row separators.

findings.description: The agenda topic headings (e.g., 'Project Finance Update', 'Project Status') appear bold in the PDF but are rendered in normal weight (not bold) in the HTML table.
findings.evidence: PDF: 'Project Finance Update', 'Project Status', 'Discuss Model Schematic', etc. are bold. HTML: these same headings appear in regular weight text within the table cells.

findings.description: The presenter names in the PDF are right-aligned on the same line as the topic headings, while in the HTML they are in a separate right column of the table, which is functionally equivalent but visually different.
findings.evidence: PDF: presenter names like '(Neelufa Sarwar)' appear right-justified inline. HTML: they appear in a dedicated 'Presenter' column.

findings.description: The PDF presents the agenda as a formatted list without explicit table structure (no visible grid/borders), while the HTML converts it into a formal table with headers 'Time', 'Topic', and 'Presenter'. The column headers are fabricated — they don't appear in the PDF.
findings.evidence: The HTML adds <thead> with <th>Time</th>, <th>Topic</th>, <th>Presenter</th> which do not appear in the PDF. However, these are reasonable structural inferences from the content layout.

findings.description: The table column headers 'Time', 'Topic', and 'Presenter' are added labels not present in the source PDF. These are structural inferences rather than substantive fabrication.
findings.evidence: PDF has no column headers; HTML adds <th>Time</th>, <th>Topic</th>, <th>Presenter</th>

findings.description: The HTML contains a nested <main> element inside another <main> element, which while not content duplication per se, indicates structural redundancy.
findings.evidence: HTML has <body><main><main>...</main></main></body> — two nested main elements.

findings.description: The document title 'AGENDA' is rendered as h1, which is acceptable. However, the heading hierarchy is flat — there are no h2 or lower headings for the agenda items, which is a reasonable simplification given the table structure used.
findings.evidence:
Only one heading:

AGENDA

. Agenda item topics are within table cells rather than headings.
